coredata.dc:title: A reusable P, N-doped carbon quantum dot fluorescent sensor for cobalt ion
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Sen
Zhu, Fawei
Zhao, Xinyi
Yang, Hua
Chen, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, phosphorus, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (P,N-CQDs) as highly selective fluorescent probe for cobalt ion (Co2+) detection were designed and synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal protocol by employing pyridoxal 5-phosphate and ethanediamine. This probe enables selective detection of Co2+ with a linear range of 0–60 μM and a limit of detection of 0.053 μM through variation in fluorescence with respect to target concentration and electron-transfer from P,N-CQDs to Co2+. Furthermore, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid-modified Fe3O4@SiO2 (Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA) was tailor-designed and prepared to successfully realize the recovery of P,N-CQDs/Co2+ fluorescence for the first time. The reusable P,N-CQDs as fluorescent sensors for Co2+ also show good linearity and sensitivity. Ultimately, this fluorescent probe was applied in the analysis of real river water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Fluorescent probe
Cobalt ions
Reusable
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of the concentration of Co2+
Recovery of fluorescence of P,N-CQDs by Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA
Cellular imaging of P,N-CQDs and dynamic observation quench fluorescence
Analysis of practical samples with P,N-CQDs and recovered P,N-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching of P,N-CQDs by Co2+
Fluorescence recovery of P,N-CQDs by Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA
Real sample detection with P,N-CQDs and recovered P,N-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation, science and Technology Department of Hunan province key project
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities of the Central South University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475152 & 21576296 ) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (No. 2017zzts339).

Hua Yang received the Ph.D. degree in Organic Chemistry in 2006 from West Virginia University, West Virginia, America. He is currently a professor and working at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Central South University, Changsha, China. He has long been committed to organic synthesis, asymmetric catalysis, and total synthesis of chiral drug molecules, hosted by the National Natural Science Foundation, science and Technology Department of Hunan province key project, participated in a number of American NSF and NIH of the fund research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for the synthesis of P,N-CQDs by PLP and DA for “off-on” Co2+ sensor.
(a) TEM images of P, N-CQDs. (b) size distribution of P,N-CQDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of P,N-CQDs. (d)FT-IR spectra of P,N-CQDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of P,N-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1 s (b), N1 s (c) and P2p(d).

Normalized FL intensity of P,N-CQDs (a) in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl; (b) at different pH; (c) continuous excitation with a 500 W Xe lamp for 60 min and (d) at room temperature for a month, respectively. (The excitation wavelength is 346 nm, the emission wavelength is 420 nm).

(a) Selectivity for various metal ions. All competing ion solutions were 0.001 M; (b) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of P,N-CQDs in the presence of increasing Co2+ concentrations (0–250 μM) in 0.01 M PBS at pH 7.0; (c) The relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and Co2+ from 0 to 250 μM; (d) Plot of (F-F0)/F0 of P,N-CQDs vs concentration of Co2+ from 0 to 60 μM; (F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities of P,N-CQDs at 420 nm in the presence or absence of Co2+, respectively).

(a) P,N-CQDs solution, P,N-CQDs with Co2+ and Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA was added into P,N-CQDs/Co2+ solution, respectively; (b) magnetic separation; (c) under 365 nm; (d) Plot of (F-F0)/F0 of recovery P,N-CQDs vs. the concentration of Co2+ from 0 to 60 μM; (e) “off-on” cycles of P,N-CQDs; (F, F0 and F1were the fluorescence intensities of P,N-CQDs at 420 nm in the presence or absence of Co2+ and after recovery, respectively).

Fluorecence images of HeLa cells excited at 346 nm, (a) bright field; (b) blue channel fluorescence images absent Co2+; (c) overlay of bright field and fluorescence images; different concentration of Co2+ ((d) 20 μM, (e) 60 μM and (f) 100 μM) added into fluorescence confocal culture dish while took photos. Scale bar: 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Determination of Co2+ in natural water samples.
Determination of Co2+ with recovered P,N-CDs in natural water samples.

head.ce:title: A reusable P, N-doped carbon quantum dot fluorescent sensor for cobalt ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Utilization of Manganese Resources, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Utilization of Manganese Resources
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, phosphorus, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (P,N-CQDs) as highly selective fluorescent probe for cobalt ion (Co2+) detection were designed and synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal protocol by employing pyridoxal 5-phosphate and ethanediamine. This probe enables selective detection of Co2+ with a linear range of 0–60 μM and a limit of detection of 0.053 μM through variation in fluorescence with respect to target concentration and electron-transfer from P,N-CQDs to Co2+. Furthermore, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid-modified Fe3O4@SiO2 (Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA) was tailor-designed and prepared to successfully realize the recovery of P,N-CQDs/Co2+ fluorescence for the first time. The reusable P,N-CQDs as fluorescent sensors for Co2+ also show good linearity and sensitivity. Ultimately, this fluorescent probe was applied in the analysis of real river water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt ion (Co2+) is essential to human body as trace amount of Co2+ acts as a stimulant for the production of red blood cells [1]. However, excess intake of Co2+ might cause harmful side effects such as paralysis, diarrhea, low blood pressure, lung irritation, bone defects, flushing, asthma, dermatitis, gastric disturbance, vasodilatation and cancer for human [2,3]. Therefore, the permissible limits of cobalt in irrigation watering and livestock watering are 0.05 and 1.0 mg L−1, respectively (Environmental Bureau of Investigation, Canadian Water Quality Guidelines [1]. On the other hand, owing to its robustness to oxidation, Co2+ is currently used in a wide array of manufacture processes such as electroplating, batty manufacturing, pigments, automotive industries, metal finishing, mining, electric cable manufacturing, coloring and catalysis. Simultaneously, the wide application of cobalt also draws environmental concern. As a result, the detection of Co2+ has gathered considerable attention ever since. For instance, MB-ATP-Cu2+ sensing of Co2+ ions on UV–vis spectra was developed, and the linear range was from 0.1 to 1.1 mM [4]. C-dots-based probe showed a wide linear response to Co2+ with a concentration ranging from 0.012 to 12 μM [5].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), nano-spherical forms of carbon, can be facilely prepared through a variety of methods, such as burning, hydrothermolysis, electrochemical oxidation, refluxing, ultrasonic and microwave treatment [6]. Over the last decade, CQDs as promising carbon materials have received extensive attention, owing to their distinct advantages such as biocompatibility [7], low toxicity [8] and high photostability [9]. In particular, CQDs are of significance in optical detection probes [10], bioimaging [11], biosensing [12], nanomedicine [13], light emitting diode materials and visible light-active photocatalysts [14,15] etc. Recently, a large number of fluorescent probes have been applied to the detection of metal ions [6,16,17]. For instance, covalent organic frameworks cutting into N-doped quantum dots (CTFQDs) were utilized as an efficient PL probe for detecting Hg2+ [18]. Selective fluorescent detection of Fe3+ in biological samples was realized by ultra-bright N/P co-doped carbon dots [19]. Furthermore, there are many reports about CQDs as “off-on” fluorescence sensor [20–22]. Yet, it is still a challenging task to recover CQDs due to the inherent hurdle for isolating CQDs from the detection system.

As well known, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) shows superior chelation and ion-exchange capability for an array of metal ions. Baker et al. found that large excess amount of EDTA was required to achieve the maximum signal recovery of PDs [23], where the removal of EDTA was problematic. Noticeably, magnetic separation has evolved as an effective separation technique due to its features of high throughput, easy manipulation and potential for automation. In particular, magnetic Fe3O4 has been found to be easily separated from the solution under a magnetic field. Recently, Dutta’s group reported a selective separation of rare earth ions from aqueous solution using functionalized magnetic nanoparticle (Cys@Fe3O4 NPs and CA@Fe3O4 NPs) [24]. We postulated that the combination of EDTA with magnetic Fe3O4 might serve as an effective solution to the unmet challenge for the recovery of CQD.

In this study, novel P,N-CQDs were synthesized by using pyridoxal 5-phosphate (PLP) and ethanediamine (DA) via a one-step hydrothermal protocol. The resulting P,N-CQDs emitting blue fluorescence with a high fluorescence quantum yield were successfully applied to selectively detect Co2+ in aqueous solution. More importantly, to address the issue for the recovery of CQDs, an innovative strategy by utilizing magnetic Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA was employed to recover P,N-CQDs from P,N-CQDs/Co2+.

Pyridoxal 5-phosphate (PLP), ethylenediaminetetraacetic dianhydride (EDTAD), and quinine sulfate were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ethanediamine (DA), NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, CoSO4, AgNO3, Cu(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3
. H2O, Zn(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, NiSO4, MgSO4, CaCl2, SrCl2, HgCl2, FeCl3
. 6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Mn(CH3COO)2, Cr(CH3COO)3, NaCl, sodium acetate, polyethylene glycol 6000, ethylene glycol and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical reagent grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm−1 by a Molecular water purification system.

Morphological evaluation and sizes of P,N-CQDs were analyzed using a HT7700 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Elements of P,N-CQDs were detected under energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, Oxford). Surface functional properties of P,N-CQDs were investigated by PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific 250Xi). Infrared spectra were measured on a pressed KBr pellet by using IR Prestige-21 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Shimadzu, Japan). Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was measured with a SDT Q600 (TA Instruments, USA) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 up to 800 °C in N2 atmosphere. Fluorescence and absorption spectra were measured with an LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, America) and a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Fluorescence lifetime was measured with Fluo Time 100 (PicoQuant, Germany).

P,N-CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using PLP and DA in Teflon lined autoclave. Initially, 531 mg (0.02 M) of PLP and 680 μL (0.01 M) of DA were mixed with 25 mL ultrapure water. And the mixture was sonicated for 15 min to give a transparent solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined auto-clave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After being cooled down to room temperature, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min to remove large dots. Finally, the resulting suspension was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 100 Da) for 24 h while ultrapure water was changed with an interval of 8 h before it was stored at 4 °C.

Quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4) was chosen as a reference at 350 nm excitation. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was calculated according to the equation:
Q X
=
Q S
(
G r a
d X
/ G r a
d S
)
(
η X 2
/
η S 2
)
Where subscripts S and X denote standard (quinine sulfate) and test samples (S,N-CQDs) respectively, Q is fluorescence quantum yield, Grad represents gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance, and η is refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the concentration of Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: P,N-CQDs was adjusted to a concentration of 10 μg mL−1 and 1 mM aqueous solutions containing thirteen different metal ions (Co2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Li+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cr3+) were prepared respectively, and then subjected to fluorescence measurements after 1 min. To study the detection range of Co2+, 1 mL of P,N-CQDs solution (20 μg mL−1 in 0.01 M PBS, pH 7.0) was added to a 2 mL EP tube, and then Co2+ aqueous solutions with different volumes were added to the system. The solutions containing different concentrations of Co2+ (0, 0.05, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 100, 150, 200 and 250 μM) were acquired by diluting with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (0.01 M, pH 7.0) to 2 mL and mixing, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 346 nm (excited at 420 nm) after 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of fluorescence of P,N-CQDs by Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: Recovery of P,N-CQDs was performed as follows: To a solution of P,N-CQDs (5 mL, 20 μg mL−1 in 0.01 M PBS, pH 7.0) in a 10 mL vial was added a solution of Co2+ (0.01 M, 200 μL) to quench the fluorescence. Then 0, 100, 200, 300 μL of Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA suspension solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was added to the mixture, respectively. The Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA was prepared according to previous reports [24,25] with some modification (for details please see ESI). Finally, certain amount of PBS solution (0.01 M, pH 7.0) was added to keep the final volume constant. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 346 nm (excited at 420 nm) after 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular imaging of P,N-CQDs and dynamic observation quench fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of P,N-CQDs in biolabeling was preliminarily evaluated by their cellular imaging in Hela cells. In brief, 100 μL of Hela cells in DMEM was supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 units per mL), and streptomycin (100 units per mL) with an initial density of 5 × 104 cells mL−1 were seeded in each hole and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The dispersion of P,N-CQDs was prepared in DMEM medium with a concentration of 50 μg mL−1. Cells were cultured with the dispersion of P,N-CQDs for 12 h and washed three times with buffer to remove free P,N-CQDs. Finally, the obtained samples were observed with a laser scanning confocal microscope. And the Co2+ solutions with varied concentrations (20 μM, 60 μM and 100 μM) were continuously added during the process of observation respectively while the photos were simultaneously taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of practical samples with P,N-CQDs and recovered P,N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Analysis of real water sample was carried out to evaluate the viability and practicality of the developed P,N-CQDs (recovered P,N-CQDs) sensor. River water samples were collected from Xiangjiang River of Changsha (China). In order to eliminate the matrix effect, the obtained real water samples were filtered through 0.22 μM filter membrane, and then diluted 5-fold with ultrapure water. To the sample of river water was added standard Co2+ solutions with different concentrations (0, 1, 2, and 4 μM), which was analyzed by utilizing the developed P,N-CQDs (recovered P,N-CQDs) sensor.

The synthetic procedure of P,N-CQDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 , in which PLP and DA act as the sources of phosphorus and nitrogen. The fluorescent efficiency of P,N-CQDs was affected by several parameters including molar ratio between PLP and DA, reaction temperature, and reaction time. Seven synthetic trials were conducted as showed in Table S1. The temperature of the hydrothermal process was also optimized. The hydrothermal process was proceeded at 180 °C for 4 h with varied ratios of PLP and DA (1:1, 2:1 and 3:1). The results demonstrated that the highest QY (15.4%) was acquired at 2:1. Therefore, the optimum conditions were finalized as follows: 0.02 mol PLP, 0.01 mol DA, 180 °C and 4 h. To the best of our knowledge, fluorescence lifetime and quantum yields (QYs) of the P,N-CQDs were much higher than most of the previously reported N,P co-doped carbon dots [26–34] (for details please see Table S2).

In order to study the structure and morphology of the prepared P,N-CQDs, a series of characterizations including TEM, fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis, FT-IR and XPS were conducted. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), uniform particle size distribution for P,N-CQDs can be observed while the average particle size of P,N-CQDs is about 4.2 nm (Fig. 1(b)).

The fluorescence lifetime of the as-prepared P,N-CQDs and P,N-CQDs/Co2+ were measured. The FL decay is mono-exponential with lifetimes of 8.30 ns (Fig. S1), and the pie chart of fluorescence lifetime shows that P,N-CQDs are pure (inset in Fig. S1). The lifetime was slightly decreased to 8.78 ns when Co2+ was added into the solution of P,N-CQDs, suggesting that the florescence quenching is a static quenching. In UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1(c)), one absorption peak at 202 nm attributed to n-σ* transition indicates the presence of NH2. And two absorption peaks at 245 and 303 nm can be assigned to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains including CC and CN bond and n-π* transition of CO bond [35,36], respectively.

As shown in Fig. 1(d), FT-IR spectrum of P,N-CQDs reveals a band around 3348 cm−1 correlated with OH and NH stretching vibrations, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 2972 cm−1 and 1418 cm−1 represent CH2 stretching vibration and CH bending vibration [37], respectively. The peak at 1605 cm−1 might be attributed to CC, CO and CN stretching in the conjugated structure [38]. The peak at 1378 cm−1 is attributed to CN stretching vibration. The stretching vibration peak of PO at 1050 cm−1 and the characteristic absorption bands of PO stretching vibration at 881 cm−1 and 650 cm−1 [19]confirm the presence of P and N elements on the surface of P,N-CQDs. The doublet peaks at 2923 cm−1 is related with the stretching vibration of aldehyde group. Presumably, all these functional groups mainly contribute to the excellent solubility of P,N-CQDs.

The results of XPS analysis are shown in Fig. 2 . Four distinct peaks around 133.0 eV, 286.0 eV, 399.0 eV, and 532.0 eV in Fig. 2(a) could be ascribed to phosphorus, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, respectively [13]. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the C1 s spectrum could be divided into five main peaks that are corresponded to CC (283.7 eV), CC (284.4 eV), CN (285.2 eV), CO (286.0 eV), and CN, CO (288.0 eV) functional groups[37,39] respectively. Deconvolution of N1 s spectra in Fig. 2(c) reveals three relative nitrogen species of CN (398.8 eV), −NH (399.6 eV) and CNC (400.2 eV) [22,40]. As shown in Fig. 2(d), two peaks at 132.4 and 133.7 eV in the P2p spectrum can be attributed to PO and PO functional groups, respectively [19,41].

The optical properties of P,N-CQDs including excitation spectrum (black line, λex = 346 nm) and several emission spectra excited by the light with different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S2. It is manifested that P,N-CQDs exhibit strong emission with λem at 420 nm, which is different from λex in a range from 316 nm to 376 nm. The effects of ionic strength, pH, illumination time and preservation time on P,N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence intensity of P,N-CQDs remained constant as the concentration of NaCl was enhanced even up to 1.0 M (Fig. 3(a)), suggesting that the prepared P,N-CQDs possess superior capability in anti-ion interference. The pH-dependent behavior of P,N-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 3(b). Notably, the highest fluorescence intensity was achieved at pH 7, suggesting that P,NCQDs can be applicable at physiologic pH. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged even after continuous excitation with a 150 W Xe lamp for 60 min. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the fluorescence intensity is still above 90% after 30 days under natural lighting conditions. All these results indicate that the prepared P,N-CQDs possess remarkable optical stability and are amenable to sensing applications under physiological conditions [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of P,N-CQDs by Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of P,N-CQDs upon addition of different metal ions were studied. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), Co2+ shows the highest selectivity towards the fluorescence quenching of P,N-CQDs, implying that P,N-CQDs could be used to selectively detect Co2+ in aqueous solution. The relationship between fluorescence intensity of P,N-CQDs and concentrations of Co2+ is shown in Fig. 4(b). The fluorescence intensity of P,N-CQDs at around 420 nm decreases gradually with the increase of Co2+ concentration, indicating that addition of Co2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of P,N-CQDs. Fig. S3 manifests the fluorescence intensities at different concentrations of Co2+, where the fluorescence intensities of P,N-CQDs weaken gradually as the concentrations of Co2+ are increased from 0 to 250 μM. As shown in Fig. 4(c), a good linear correlation (y = 0.00495x + 0.00937) over the range from 0 to 60 μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.993 can be obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.053 μM (LOD = 3σ/S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of P,N-CQDs by Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: According to TG analysis shown in Fig. S4(a), more than 40% of weight loss for Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA occurred at 250–300 °C, partially confirming the modification of EDTA on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2. And as shown in Fig. S4(b), FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA reveals a band around 2936 cm−1 correlated with CH2 stretching vibration. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks at 1765 cm−1 and 1730 cm−1 represent carboxylic acid and carboxylic ester of CO stretching, respectively. Accordingly, it can be tentatively concluded that EDTA has been successfully attached to the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a)–(c), the fluorescence can be effectively recovered by the addition of Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA and the following magnetic separation. As a result, when 200 μL Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA suspension solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was added into P,N-CQDs/Co2+ solution, 70% of the original signal for P,N-CQDs was recovered. And the fluorescence recovery of P,N-CQDs was also proven viable even after five cycles (Fig. 5(d)). Presumably, different chelation modes contribute to the binding of Co2+ ions on the surface of P,N-CQDs, possibly causing the difficulty in the complete removal of Co2+ from P,N-CQDs/Co2+. It is worth mentioning that a good linear relationship ranging from 0 to 60 μM was also observed by using the recovered P,N-CQDs solution (Fig. 5(e)). Gratifyingly, the recovered P,N-CQDs show high sensitivity in the detection of cobalt ions without being affected by the incomplete recovery of fluorescence. Essentially, it is the first time to realize the isolation of the recovered CQDS based on the results of literature survey (Table S3) [5].

Living cells were imaged with a confocal microscope under bright-field illumination (Fig. 6 (a)). The confocal image shows blue emission in HeLa cells under 346 nm laser excitation (Fig. 6(b)). With the increase in the concentration of Co2+ (20 μM, 60 μM and 100 μM, respectively), the blue emissions gradually weaken as displayed in Fig. 6(d)–(f). These results suggest that P,N-CQDs are able to pass through cell membranes and enter cells readily, showing satisfactory cell permeability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample detection with P,N-CQDs and recovered P,N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Finally, practical application of P,N-CQDs and recovered P,N-CQDs as fluorescence probes were exploited respectively. Standard addition experiments were performed with different aqueous samples to validate this method, and the results are shown in Tables 1 and 2
. As shown in Table 1, the relative standard deviation (RSD) is lower than 0.72 and the recoveries are ranged from 96.6% to 109.3% for the samples from Xiangjiang River in Changsha. As shown in Table 2, the sensitivity of the recovered P,N-CQDs in the detection of Co2+ can be persistently maintained, implying that P,N-CQDs can be effectively recycled by Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA. These results further exemplify the potential of the P,N-CQDs-based fluorescent probe in the selective detection of Co2+.

In conclusion, P,N-CQDs with specifically optical stabilities was first synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method, which were thoroughly characterized via chemical composition and structural analysis. The as-prepared P,N-CQDs were utilized to selectively detect Co2+ as the fluorescence intensity of P,N-CQDs can be effectively quenched by Co2+. More importantly, an innovative strategy for recovering CQDs was proposed and successfully realized by employing the tailor-made Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA. As a consequence, P,N-CQDs could be readily reused in the detection of Co2+ through simple magnetic separation, which conceptually exemplifies the reuse of carbon quantum dots.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475152 & 21576296) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (No. 2017zzts339).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.206.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient detection and extraction of cobalt(II) from lithium ion batteries and wastewater by novel composite adsorbent
Rapid removal of cobalt ion from aqueous solutions by almond green hull
Solvent extraction of cobalt(II) ions; cooperation of oximes and neutral donors
A simple and novel system for colorimetric detection of cobalt ions
Enhanced photoresponsive polyethyleneimine/citric acid co-carbonized dots for facile and selective sensing and intracellular imaging of cobalt ions at physiologic pH
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for off-on sensing of Hg(II) and I(−)
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Bi-functional fluorescent polymer dots: a one-step synthesis via controlled hydrothermal treatment and application as probes for the detection of temperature and Fe3+
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticle modulated turn-on fluorescent probes for histidine detection and its imaging in living cells
Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
High fluorescence S, N co-doped carbon dots as an ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of uric acid
Mesoporous TiO2 modified with carbon quantum dots as a high-performance visible light photocatalyst
Superior nanoporous graphitic carbon nitride photocatalyst coupled with CdS quantum dots for photodegradation of RhB
Graphene quantum dot membranes as fluorescent sensing platforms for Cr (VI) detection
Carbon quantum dots prepared with polyethyleneimine as both reducing agent and stabilizer for synthesis of Ag/CQDs composite for Hg2+ ions detection
Rapid exfoliation of layered covalent triazine-based frameworks into N-doped quantum dots for the selective detection of Hg2+ ions
Highly Fe3+-selective fluorescent nanoprobe based on ultra-bright N/P co-doped carbon dots and its application in biological samples
Fluorescent probes for off-on highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ and L-cysteine based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots
A carbon dot-based off-on fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of phytic acid
Bright-yellow-emissive N-doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing
Pee-dots: biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots derived from the upcycling of urine
Selective separation of rare earth ions from aqueous solution using functionalized magnetite nanoparticles: kinetic and thermodynamic studies
Design of Fe3O4@SiO2@carbon quantum dot based nanostructure for fluorescence sensing magnetic separation, and live cell imaging of fluoride ion
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for the highly sensitive detection of Ag+ and cell imaging
Water-soluble, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution
One-pot solid phase pyrolysis synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe 3+ sensing and bioimaging
Facile synthesis of P-doped carbon quantum dots with highly efficient photoluminescence
Phosphorus, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species inside macrophages
Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots as a nano-sensor for highly sensitive and selective imaging detection of nitrite in live cell
Fabrication of nitrogen- and phosphorous-doped carbon dots by the pyrolysis method for iodide and iron(III) sensing
Low temperature synthesis of phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped yellow fluorescent carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging
One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells
Triple-mode emission of carbon dots: applications for advanced anti-counterfeiting
Multifunctional N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots with pH- and thermo-dependent switchable fluorescent properties and highly selective detection of glutathione
On-resonance fluorescence, resonance rayleigh scattering, and ratiometric resonance synchronous spectroscopy of molecular- and quantum dot-fluorophores
Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid
Synthesis and formation mechanistic investigation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high quantum yields and yellowish-green fluorescence
An on-off-on fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sen Liao is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Central South University, Changsha, China. His current research interests are focused on the preparation and application of quantum dots (carbon dots, metallic quantum dots and semiconducting quantum dots).

Fa-wei Zhu is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Central South University, Changsha, China. His research interests include the modification and functionalization of carbon materials (carbon nanotubes, graphene, and graphene nanoribbons) for the application of adsoption, preconcentration and separation techniques, and electrochemical sensors.

Xin-yi Zhao is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Central South University, Changsha, China. His current research interests are focused on the preparation and application of quantum dots (carbon dots, metallic quantum dots and semiconducting quantum dots).

Hua Yang received the Ph.D. degree in Organic Chemistry in 2006 from West Virginia University, West Virginia, America. He is currently a professor and working at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Central South University, Changsha, China. He has long been committed to organic synthesis, asymmetric catalysis, and total synthesis of chiral drug molecules, hosted by the National Natural Science Foundation, science and Technology Department of Hunan province key project, participated in a number of American NSF and NIH of the fund research work.

Xiao-Qing Chen received the Ph.D. degree in Applied Chemistry in 2006 from Central South University, Changsha, China. She is currently a professor and working at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Central South University, Changsha, China, and she is a senior engineer and working at Collaborative Innovation Center of Resource-conserving & Environment-friendly Society and Ecological Civilization, Changsha, China. She has been active in the investigation of several aspects of the application of carbon-based materials. Her research interests are in the preparation of quantum dots fluorescent probe (carbon dots, metallic quantum dots and semiconducting quantum dots). for the application of fluorescent detection, biosensing and electrochemical sensors.
